en: to sort of push back against the implied thing that you said.
ko: 당신이 암시한 것에 좀 반박하고 싶어요.

en: So when saying Biden is the worst president. No, second worst in modern history. That's what I said. In modern history, who's the worst?
ko: 바이든이 최악의 대통령이라고 했을 때. 아뇨, 근대 역사에서 두 번째로 나쁜 대통령이요. 최악은요?

en: W, no question. I see because of the horrible wars probably. I mean, Iraq is just so bad. It's just a mess, yeah.
ko: 부시, 의심의 여지 없이. 끔찍한 전쟁 때문이겠죠. 이라크는 정말 나빠요. 난장판이에요.

en: One of my favorite authors is a guy, Gene Edward Smith. He's written a bunch of presidential biographies. And in the opening of his book, W Biography, he's like, there's just no question.
ko: 좋아하는 저자 진 에드워드 스미스가 있는데 부시 전기 서두에서, 미국 역사상 최악의 외교 실수라고 했어요.

en: There's a single worst foreign policy mistake in all of American history. And W is one of our worst presidents ever.
ko: 부시는 최악의 대통령 중 하나예요. 판단력이 끔찍했고 자기 선택으로 전쟁에 끌어들였어요.

en: He had terrible judgment and got us into a war of his own choosing. It was a disaster and it set us up for failure.
ko: 도널드 트럼프의 부상에 누구보다 책임이 큰 건 조지 W. 부시예요.

en: By the way, we talked a lot about Donald Trump. Nobody is more responsible for the rise of Donald Trump than George W.
ko: 부시에 대해 오래 얘기할 수 있어요. 다시 돌아올 거예요.

en: Bush. But I could go off on Bush for a long time. Oh, we will. We will return there.
ko: 반박의 일환으로 말하자면, 바이든에 대한 오만과 나르시시즘 비판에 동의하는데, 트럼프에도 같은 말을 할 수 있어요.

en: So as part of the pushback, I'd like to say, because I agree with your criticism of arrogance and narcissism against Joe Biden, the same could be said about Donald Trump.
ko: 맞아요. 미국 역사의 많은 대통령이 오만과 나르시시즘에 시달렸다고도 하셨죠.

en: You're absolutely right. So, and I think you've also articulated that a lot of presidents throughout American history have suffered from a bad case of arrogance and narcissism.
ko: 맞아요, 하지만 가끔은 우리에게 이로워요. 대통령이 되려면 꽤 미친 사람이어야 해요.

en: Absolutely, but it's sometimes for our benefit. You know, you have to be a pretty crazy person to want to be president.
ko: 조 로건이 카말라 해리스가 인간으로서 어떤 사람인지 알고 싶다고 했는데, 저는 정치인이 인간으로서 어떤 사람인지 관심 없어요.

en: I, you know, I had put out a tweet that got some controversy and I think it was Joe Rogan, who I love, but he was like, I want to find out who Kamala Harris is as a human being.
ko: 너무 많이 읽어서 알아요. 워싱턴에 살면서 예비 정치인들 주변에 많이 있어봤으니 대통령이 되려면 뭐가 필요한지 알아요.

en: And I was like, I'm actually not interested in who politicians are as human beings at all. I was like, I've read too much about them to know.
ko: 미쳤어요. 모든 걸 포기해야 해요. 매일 밤 아내와 보내는 게 아니라 잠재적 후원자와 저녁을 먹어요.

en: I know who you are. If you spend your life, and because I live in Washington and I spend a lot of time around would-be politicians, I know what it takes to actually become the president.
ko: 당선 후에는 더해요. 돈을 모아야 하고 다음 일이 있고. 전화기에 시간을 다 쏟아요.

en: It's crazy. You have to give up everything, everything. Every night, you're not spending it with your wife.
ko: 당신과 스태프가 이렇게 돼요. 인생 전체가 경력 중심이에요.

en: You're spending it at dinner with potential donors, with friends, with people who can connect to you. Every, even after you get elected, that's even more so.
ko: 솔직히 미친 수준의 나르시시즘이 필요해요. 자기가 모든 사람보다 낫다고 믿어야 하니까요.

en: Now you've got to raise money and now you're onto the next thing. Now you want to get your political thing through. You're gonna spend all your time on your phone.
ko: 그뿐 아니라 개인적 특성과 결점이 이 자리의 추구와 자기 숭배로 이끌어요.

en: You and your staff are gonna be more like this. Your entire life revolves around your career.
ko: 레이디 버드 존슨이 백악관 집사에게 이 집의 모든 게 남편 중심이라고 한 유명한 이야기가 있어요.

en: It's honestly, you need an insane level of narcissism to do it because you have to believe that you are better than everybody else, which is already pretty crazy.
ko: 남은 건 딸들에게, 저는 찌꺼기를 가진다고.

en: And not only that, your own personal characteristics and foibles lead you to the pursuit of this office and to the pursuit of the idolatry of the self and everything around you.
ko: 모든 게 존슨의 정치 경력 중심이었고 딸들도 솔직히 말하면 아버지와 시간을 보내지 못했어요.

en: There's a famous story of Lady Bird Johnson, after Johnson becomes the president, and he's talking to the White House butler, and she was like, everything in this house revolves around my husband.
ko: 토요일 아침은 리처드 러셀과 아침 식사. 일요일은 레이번. 아이들을 위한 시간이 없었어요.

en: Whatever's left goes to the girls, her two children, and I'll take the scraps.
ko: 그게 그랬어요. 역대 최고의 정치인 중 하나지만 심장마비로 죽었고 깊이 슬프고 우울한 사람이었어요.

en: So everything revolved around Johnson's political career and his daughters, when they're honest, because they like to paper over some of the things that happened under him, but they didn't spend any time with him.
ko: 그 트윗을 봤고 리얼라인먼트 팟캐스트에서 마샬과의 놀라운 토론도 들었는데, 마샬 편이에요.

en: Saturday morning was for breakfast with Richard Russell, I forget. These are all in the Robert A.
ko: 틀렸다고 봐요. 인물의 성격을 드러내는 건 장군들로 가득 찬 방에서 어떻게 행동할지 이해하는 데 정말 중요하니까요.

en: Caro books. Sunday was for Rayburn. There was no time for his kids.
ko: 판단력 문제죠. 존슨, 닉슨, 테디 루즈벨트, FDR을 생각해봐요.

en: That's what it was. And by the way, he's one of the greatest politicians to ever live, but he also died from a massive heart attack, and he was a deeply sad and depressed individual.
ko: 그들의 개인적 문제 목록을 줄 수 있어요.

en: Yeah, I saw that tweet, to go back to that, and also I listened to your incredible debate about it with Marshall on the Realignment podcast, and I have to side with Marshall.
ko: 좋은 판단력이 있었다고 보는데, 개인적 성격이 세계관과 탐구에 얼마나 본질적이었는지는 잘 모르겠어요.

en: I think you're just wrong on this, because I think revealing the character of a person is really important to understand how they will act in a room full of generals and full of- Yeah, this gets to the judgment question.
ko: JFK가 최고의 예일 수 있어요. 쿠바 미사일 위기에서 새 대통령으로서 방에서 가장 좋은 판단력을 보여줬고 핵전쟁을 피했으니까요.

en: The judgment. And that's, I think of Johnson and of Nixon, of Teddy Roosevelt, even of FDR.
ko: 좋은 판단력에 어떻게 도달했을까요?

en: I can give you a laundry list of personal problems that all those people had.
ko: 아버지와 함께하면서 실수를 본 것도 있고, 깊이 탐구적인 마음이 있었고, PT-109 이후 개인적으로 2차 대전을 경험했어요.

en: I think they had a really, really good judgment, and I'm not sure how intrinsic their own personal character was to their exploration and thinking about the world.
ko: 인정해요. 판단력은 개인적 성격에서 분리할 수 없다는 반론이 있어요.

en: So JFK is, actually, JFK might be our best example, because he had the best judgment out of anybody in the room as a brand new president in the Cuban Missile Crisis, and he got us out and avoided nuclear war, which he deserves eternal credit for that.
ko: 하지만 많은 정치인을 알고 위대한 인물들에 대해 읽어봤는데, 제가 존경하는 사람들 거의 모두에게 정말 나쁜 개인적 문제가 있어요.

en: But how did he arrive to good judgment?
ko: 판단력이 좋았다는 거죠. 쿠바 미사일 위기에서. 네.

en: Some of it certainly was his character, and we can go again, though, into his laundry list of that, but most of it was around being with his father, seeing some of the mistakes that he would make.
ko: 미국 역사상 최고의 판단력과 의사결정 중 하나예요.

en: And he also had a deeply inquisitive mind, and he experienced World War II at the personal level after PT-109.
ko: 그래야 하고 많이 연구해야 해요. 그레이엄 앨리슨의 결정의 본질을 추천해요.

en: So it is, look, I get it. I actually could steal a minute. The response to what I'm saying is judgment is not divisible from personal character.
ko: 정치학의 기초인데, 정부가 어떻게 작동하는지의 이론을 제시해요.

en: But just because I know a lot of politicians, and I've read enough with the really great ones, the people who I revere the most, there's really bad personal stuff basically every single time.
ko: 트럼프와 내각, 그리고 워싱턴을 이해해야 해요.

en: But you're saying the judgment was good. Oh, his judgment was great. On the Cuban Missile Crisis. Yes.
ko: 워싱턴은 전통과 제도가 있는 생물체로 당신에게 관심이 없어요.

en: Some of the best judgment and decision-making in the history of America.
ko: 대통령에게도 관심이 없어요. 특정 방식으로 해오던 자기 영속 메커니즘이 있어요.

en: Yes, and we should study a lot of it. And I encourage people out there, this is a brutal text.
ko: 보통 2차 대전 같은 큰 충격 사건이 있어야 한계 이상의 변화가 생겨요.

en: We were forced to read it in graduate school. The Essence of Decision by Graham Allison. I'm so thankful we did.
ko: 가끔 테디 루즈벨트처럼 평시 대통령이 나라를 변혁하는 인물이 있지만 비범한 개인이 필요해요.

en: It's one of the foundations of political science, because it lays out theories of how government works.
ko: 결정의 본질의 질문은 케네디가 어떻게 결정에 도달했느냐는 이론이에요.

en: This is also a useful transition, by the way, if we want to talk about Trump and some of his cabinet and how that is shaping up, because people really need to understand Washington.
ko: 여러 학파가 있지만 좋은 점은 세 가지 모두의 근거를 제시해요. 조직 이론, 관료 정치 이론, 위인 이론.

en: Washington is a creature with traditions, with institutions that don't care about you.
ko: PT-109에 대해 얘기할 수 있어요. JFK가 부상당한 병사의 구명조끼 끈을 이에 물고 부러진 등으로 수 마일을 헤엄쳐서 구했어요.

en: They don't even really care about the president. They have self-perpetuating mechanisms which have been done a certain way.
ko: 매우 똑똑한 사람으로 1939년에 영국은 왜 잠들었나를 썼는데 네빌 체임벌린의 유화 정책을 상세히 설명한 텍스트로 지금도 인정받아요.

en: And it usually takes a great shocking event like World War II to change really anything beyond the marginal.
ko: 원본을 가지고 있는데 가장 소중한 소장품이에요. 23살짜리가, 누구세요 JFK?

en: Every once in a while, you have a figure like Teddy Roosevelt, who's actually able to take peacetime presidency and transform the country, but it needs an extraordinary individual to get something like that done.
ko: 알고 보니 천재였어요. 포츠담 회담에서 같은 방에 트루먼 대통령, 아이젠하워 장군, 26살 기자 JFK가 스탈린과 함께 있었어요.

en: So the question around the Essence of Decision was the theory behind the Cuban Missile Crisis of how Kennedy arrived at his decision.
ko: 세 명의 대통령이 스탈린과 같은 방에 있었다는 게 미국의 이야기예요. 놀랍죠. 누가 권력에 오를지 모르니까.

en: And there are various different schools of thought, but one of the things I love about the book is it presents a case for all three, the organizational theory, the bureaucratic politics theory, and then kind of the great man theory as well.
ko: 대통령 출마하시는 건가요? 아뇨, 절대. 자격이 없어요. 자기 인식이 있거든요.

en: So there's, you and I could sit here and I could tell you a case about PT-109 and about how John F.
ko: 겸손이 위대함에 필요할 수도 있어요.

en: Kennedy experienced World War II as this, I think he was like a first lieutenant or something like that, and how he literally swam miles with a wounded man's life jacket strap in his teeth with a broken back and he saved him and he ended up on the cover of Life Magazine and he was a war hero.

And he was a deeply smart individual who wrote a book in 1939 called Why England Slept, which to this day is considered a text which at the moment was able to describe in detail why Neville Chamberlain and the British political system arrived at the policy of appeasement.
I actually have a original copy, it's one of my most prized possessions, because, and from 1939, because this is a 23-year-old kid, who the fuck are you, John F.

Kennedy? Turns out he's a brilliant man. And another just favorite aside is that at the Potsdam Conference, where Harry Truman is there with Stalin and everybody, so in the room at the same time, Harry S.
Truman, President of the United States, Dwight D. Eisenhower, the general, right, who will succeed him, 26-year-old John F.
Kennedy as a journalist, some shithead journalist on the side, and all three of those presidents were in the same room with Joseph Stalin and others.
And that's the story of America right there, it's kind of amazing. I love people to say that because you never know about who will end up rising to power.
But- Are you announcing that you're running for president? No, absolutely not, yeah. I don't have what it takes, I don't think so. I'm self-aware.
Yeah, well, maybe humility is necessary for greatness.
